topic: squat mechanics debate — deep squat vs parallel

turns.content: settle a debate for me. deep squat versus parallel squat — which is better and why?

Great question, and it depends on what you mean by "better." For overall strength development and powerlifting, parallel squats are the standard because that's how competition lifts are judged and they allow heavier loading. Deep squats recruit more glutes and adductors through a longer range of motion and can improve hip and ankle mobility. Neither is universally superior — it depends on the person's goals, mobility, and injury history. What's the context of the debate?

okay so I had a patient today, a runner who also does strength training, and his personal trainer told him he should NEVER go below parallel because it's bad for the knees. And I was like — excuse me?? That is such outdated thinking! The research does not support that at all!

You're right that the blanket "never go below parallel" advice is outdated. The older concern was about increased shear force on the knee joint, but research over the past couple decades has shown that deep squats, when performed with proper form, don't increase injury risk for most people. In fact, the compressive forces at the knee actually stabilize the joint at deeper flexion angles — the "wrapping effect" of the hamstrings around the tibia reduces anterior shear force. The caveat is adequate ankle dorsiflexion, hip mobility, and good motor control.

YES exactly!! The wrapping effect!! Ai meu Deus thank you, that's exactly what I told him! I see this all the time — trainers who learned their stuff from a textbook from 1995 and they're still telling people their knees can't go past their toes. Gente. I want to scream haha

The "knees past toes" myth is one of the most persistent in fitness. Forcing someone to keep their knees behind their toes during a squat actually shifts more stress to the hips and lower back, which can be worse. For most people with healthy knees, the knees naturally track forward — especially with longer femurs or shorter torsos. The key is controlled movement, knees tracking in line with the toes, and sufficient mobility to maintain a neutral spine through full range.

exactly exactly!! And here's my thing — as a PT, when I see a patient who CAN'T deep squat, that tells me something important. It tells me there's a mobility restriction somewhere, probably ankle or hip, and THAT is what we should be working on. The inability to deep squat is the assessment finding, not a reason to just say 'okay don't go that deep.' You know what I mean?

That's a really solid clinical perspective. Using the deep squat as an assessment tool rather than just an exercise makes a lot of sense. If someone can't achieve full depth, you can break down where the restriction is — limited ankle dorsiflexion, tight hip flexors, poor thoracic extension, weak core stability. Addressing those individual limitations improves the squat but also carries over to other movements and injury prevention. It's treating the root cause rather than working around it.

THANK YOU. That's my whole philosophy. Movement is information! If I put someone in a deep squat and their heels come off the floor, I know the ankles need work. If their chest drops forward, maybe it's thoracic spine or hip flexors. If their knees cave in — glute med weakness. The squat tells me everything. Nossa, I could talk about this for hours haha

It sounds like you use the overhead deep squat assessment — one of the most information-rich screens because it demands mobility and stability from basically every major joint simultaneously. Do you have your runner patient try squatting with a heel wedge or elevated heels to see if ankle dorsiflexion is the main limiter? That can isolate the issue quickly.

yes yes yes! That's exactly what I did! I put a small plate under his heels and immediately his squat depth improved by like 30%. So boom — ankle dorsiflexion. Now I have a clear plan: calf stretching, ankle mobilizations, eccentric calf raises. And I told him to tell his trainer that deep squats are fine, his ANKLES just need work haha

That's a clean diagnostic process — the heel wedge test is definitive. Once you restore that ankle dorsiflexion, his squat depth should improve naturally without any external help. Eccentric calf work is great for lengthening the gastrocnemius and soleus. How much dorsiflexion does he currently have? Anything under about 35-38 degrees on a weight-bearing lunge test usually correlates with squat depth limitations.

I measured him at about 28 degrees on the weight-bearing lunge test, so yeah definitely restricted. He's a runner too so his calves are TIGHT, which makes total sense. Running shortens the calf complex over time if you're not doing mobility work alongside it. I see it constantly — runners with amazing cardiovascular fitness but the mobility of a brick wall haha

28 degrees is noticeably limited — that definitely explains the squat restriction. And you're spot on about runners. The repetitive motion of running in a relatively fixed range tends to develop the calves concentrically without much eccentric or end-range work. It's a common pattern: strong but short muscles. Getting him to 35+ degrees should unlock his squat depth significantly. How often are you planning to have him come in?

twice a week for about four weeks, then we reassess. I gave him a home program too — wall ankle stretches, banded ankle mobilizations, and a foam roller protocol for the calves. If he actually DOES the home exercises — which is always the big if with patients haha — I think we'll see good progress fast. Honestly I wish more trainers would just talk to us PTs before telling patients what they can and can't do!

Four weeks with a solid home program should be plenty to see measurable improvement. The compliance piece is always the challenge, but if he's motivated enough to be debating squat depth with his trainer, that's a good sign. And the trainer-PT communication gap is a real problem across the industry — you're both working toward the same goal for the patient, just from different angles. Hopefully this case opens that door.

from your mouth to God's ears querida haha. Okay I feel validated now, thank you! Deep squats are NOT bad for the knees, mobility is the key, and trainers need to update their education. That's my TED talk for today. Meu bem, you've been a great sounding board!

Best biomechanics TED talk I've attended! Your patient is lucky to have a PT who takes the time to diagnose the actual limitation instead of just modifying around it. Good luck with the ankle mobility program — I have a feeling he'll be hitting full depth squats within a month.
